coredata.dc:title: Surface PEGylation and biological imaging of fluorescent Tb3+-doped layered double hydroxides through the photoinduced RAFT polymerization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Fengjie
Zhou, Huajian
Chen, Junyu
Huang, Hongye
Tian, Jianwen
Wen, Yuanqing
Huang, Qiang
Liu, Meiying
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Tb3+-doped layered double hydroxides (LDHs) exhibit excellent optical characteristics, uniform size and uniform morphologies when synthesized through a hydrothermal method. However, due to their lack of functional groups and poor dispersibility, applications of these fluorescent Tb3+-doped LDHs have been largely impeded especially in the biomedical fields. In this work, a novel strategy was developed for the surface modification of these fluorescent Tb3+-doped LDHs using photoinduced surface-initiated reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization with hydrophilic poly(ethylene glycol) methacrylate (PEGMA) as the monomer. The final products were obtained via the metal free surface-initiated RAFT polymerization with light irradiation. Successful preparation of these fluorescent LDHs polymer composites (LDH-PEG) was confirmed by a number of analytical technologies, such as transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and thermogravimetric analysis. In addition, laser scanning confocal microscope was employed to examine the cell uptake behavior of the LDH-PEG composites and evaluate their potential for biomedical applications. We demonstrated that the hydrophilic monomer PEGMA could be facilely grafted on the surface of Tb3+-doped LDHs through metal free photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. The resultant LDH-PEG composites displayed high water dispersibility, strong fluorescence, low cytotoxicity and a desirable cell uptake performance. These features of the LDH-PEG composites indicated their great potential for biomedical applications. More importantly, photoinduced RAFT polymerization has the advantages of a conventional controlled living radical polymerization, which could overcome drawbacks such as toxicity, the fluorescence quenching effects of metal catalysts and the complex synthesis of chain transfer agents. Therefore, this method could be an alternative tool for the surface modification of materials and fabrication of multifunctional fluorescent nanomaterials based polymer composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent Tb3+-doped LDHs
Surface PEGylation
Biological imaging
Metal free photoinduced polymerization
Surface-initiated RAFT polymerization

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of –NH2 terminated LDH (aminated LDH named LDH-NH2)
Synthesis of LDH-Cl intermediate (surface modified with -Cl group)
Evaluation of the cell viability of LDH-PEG composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016 , 21474057 , 21564006 , 21561022 , 21644014 , 51673107 , 21788102 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072 , 20161BAB213066 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of LDH, LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG composites. The characteristic absorption peaks at 2910 and 1108 cm−1 were enhanced in LDH-PEG composites samples. These peaks could be attributed to the C-H and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively.
XRD patterns of MgAlTb-LDH (A) and LDH-PEG(B) in the range of 2θ = 2°–80°.

TGA curves of LDHs, LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG composites. A significant weight loss was observed between 320 and 410 °C in the TGA curve of the LDH-PEG composites. The total weight loss of the LDH-PEG composites is notably greater than that of the unmodified LDHs. We determined that the LDHs weight loss was 25.8% while that of LDH-NH2 increased to 35.8% and those of LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG were 49.4%, 48.3% and 51.3% respectively based on the TGA data. Based on these results, the 35.5% weight loss observed for LDH-PEG resulted from decomposition of the PEGMA monomer. The TGA curves clearly confirmed that LDHs could be successfully modified with the PEGMA hydrophilic monomer through photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization.
TEM images of Tb3+-doped LDHs and their composites. (A) Tb3+-doped LDHs, (B) TEM image of the LDH-PEG composites, scale bar = 100 nm.
Survey XPS spectra of Tb3+-doped LDHs, LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG. Characteristic elements, such as S were found in the LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG composites samples.
High resolution XPS spectra of LDHs, LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG. (A) The C 1s region, (B) the N 1s region, (C) the S 2p region, (D) the Cl 2p region.
Fluorescence spectra of LDH-PEG composites. The LDH-PEG composites maintain the fluorescence properties of unmodified Tb3+-doped LDHs.

Cell viability of LDH-PEG composites towards L929 cells. Cells were incubated with different concentrations of LDH-PEG composites for 12 and 24 h. The cell viability values were determined using CCK-8 assay.

CLSM images of LDH-PEG composites after the cells were incubated with 100 µg mL−1 of LDH-PEG composites for 3 h. The cells were excited with a laser with wavelength of 488 nm. (A) Bright field image, (B) fluorescent image and (C) overlaid image of A and B.
Schematic showing the preparation of PEGylated Tb3+-doped luminescent LDHs through the metal free photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization.
Elemental contents (%) based on XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Surface PEGylation and biological imaging of fluorescent Tb3+-doped layered double hydroxides through the photoinduced RAFT polymerization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanotechnology and Institute of Biomedical Technology, Chung-Yuan Christian University, Chung-Li 32023, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanotechnology and Institute of Biomedical Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China (Y. Wei).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tb3+-doped layered double hydroxides (LDHs) exhibit excellent optical characteristics, uniform size and uniform morphologies when synthesized through a hydrothermal method. However, due to their lack of functional groups and poor dispersibility, applications of these fluorescent Tb3+-doped LDHs have been largely impeded especially in the biomedical fields. In this work, a novel strategy was developed for the surface modification of these fluorescent Tb3+-doped LDHs using photoinduced surface-initiated reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization with hydrophilic poly(ethylene glycol) methacrylate (PEGMA) as the monomer. The final products were obtained via the metal free surface-initiated RAFT polymerization with light irradiation. Successful preparation of these fluorescent LDHs polymer composites (LDH-PEG) was confirmed by a number of analytical technologies, such as transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and thermogravimetric analysis. In addition, laser scanning confocal microscope was employed to examine the cell uptake behavior of the LDH-PEG composites and evaluate their potential for biomedical applications. We demonstrated that the hydrophilic monomer PEGMA could be facilely grafted on the surface of Tb3+-doped LDHs through metal free photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. The resultant LDH-PEG composites displayed high water dispersibility, strong fluorescence, low cytotoxicity and a desirable cell uptake performance. These features of the LDH-PEG composites indicated their great potential for biomedical applications. More importantly, photoinduced RAFT polymerization has the advantages of a conventional controlled living radical polymerization, which could overcome drawbacks such as toxicity, the fluorescence quenching effects of metal catalysts and the complex synthesis of chain transfer agents. Therefore, this method could be an alternative tool for the surface modification of materials and fabrication of multifunctional fluorescent nanomaterials based polymer composites.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of novel tools to analyze the cell morphology and protein expression processes is of great importance for the advancement of biology and biomedicine [1]. In this context, optical biological imaging, one of the most popular biological imaging technologies, was selected as the best method because of its high sensitivity, high contrast and easy operation [2–7]. Traditionally, organic fluorochromes, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), fluorescent metal clusters, carbon/silicon quantum dots (CQDs), lanthanide ions doped nanomaterials and fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles have been extensively explored [4,8–13]. However, organic compounds are hydrophobic in nature and therefore are difficult to disperse in aqueous solution. Because CQDs can emit different wavelengths under different excitation wavelengths, they are unreliable and easily influenced by optical pollution [14–16]. Some other fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials are typically non-degradable and highly toxic because of their chemical compositions. Compared with other fluorescent probes, lanthanide ions doped nanomaterials such as hydroxyapatite and layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have recently attracted the most attention for their advantageous low cost, easy preparation, unique optical properties, biodegradability and biocompatibility [17]. Although much progress has been made in the synthesis of these lanthanide ions doped nanomaterials, their biomedical applications are still in the initial stages because of their poor water dispersibility and lack of functional groups. The surface modification of lanthanide ions doped hydroxyapatite has been investigated over the past few years, however, the surface modification of lanthanide ions LDHs and the potential biomedical applications of these modified fluorescent LDHs remains largely unexplored.

LDHs are a class of synthetic anionic clays whose structure is based on brucite (Mg(OH)2)-like layers in which part of the divalent cations are replaced by other trivalent ions to give positively-charged sheets. This positive charge is balanced by intercalated anions located in the interlayer regions. Furthermore, the intensities and ratios of the di- and trivalent cations and the interlayer anion can be varied over a wide range, giving rise to a large series of isostructural materials [18,19]. Because of their excellent absorption capacity and special stratified structure, LDHs have already attracted tremendous interest in various fields such as biology, medicine, chemistry, catalysis and so on [20]. The characteristic intrinsic anion exchange characteristic and special interlayer tunneling structures allow different functionalized LDHs to be obtained [21–23]. Recently, different types of functionalized LDHs have been extensively investigated for several practical applications in various fields [24–29], including flame retardancy [30–35], adsorption [36–40], catalysis [41–45], biomedicine [46,47] and so on. Although some previous reports have demonstrated the synthesis of lanthanide ions doped fluorescent LDHs and their applications in biomedical fields, the unmodified lanthanide ions doped fluorescent LDHs is largely impeded by their poor water dispersibility and lack of functional groups. Metal free photoinduced surface-initiated controlled living polymerization is a useful tool for the surface modification of materials to give polymer composites with novel properties and functions. However, to our knowledge, no reports have demonstrated the surface modification of lanthanide ions doped fluorescent LDHs through photoinduced surface-initiated controlled living radical polymerization [48].

In this work, Tb3+-doped luminescent LDHs functionalized with hydrophilic polymers were prepared through photoinduced surface-initiated reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization with poly(ethylene glycol) methacrylate (PEGMA) as the monomer. As shown in Scheme 1 , Tb3+ was incorporated into the layers of LDHs through hydrothermal treatment and the resulting Tb3+-doped LDHs emitted green fluorescence. Subsequently, amino groups were introduced to the surface of the LDHs through surface functionalization with 3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), and the amines could be used to immobilize the chain transfer agent (CTA). Finally, the hydrophilic monomer PEGMA was grafted onto the surface of Tb3+-doped LDHs through photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. The successful grafting of these polymers on the surface of luminescent LDHs through photoinduced RAFT polymerization was confirmed by a number of characterization techniques, and the potential biomedical applications of these luminescent LDHs-based polymer composites were further evaluated based on studies of the cell viability and cell uptake.

Terbium (III) chloride hexahydrate (TbCl3·6H2O, 99.9%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, 96%), anhydrous N,N-Dimethylformamide (dry DMF, 99.9%, further dried with molecular sieves, water ≤50 ppm) and toluene (AR, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochem LLC. Aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O, AR, 97%). Anhydrous magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, AR), sodium diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate (AR, 99%), poly-(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA, Mr = 950), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, 98%) and 4-(chloromethyl) benzoyl chloride (98%) were all provided by the Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit microscope operated at 120 kV to investigate the surface morphology of Tb3+-doped LDHs. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer using an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) to ascertain the major elements and analyze the corresponding chemical bonds in the LDHs, LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG respectively. All binding energies were calibrated using the contaminant carbon (C 1s = 284.6 eV) as a charge reference. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using a Nicolet 380 Fourier transform spectrometer instrument in transmission mode ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 2 cm−1 to detect the characteristic functional groups in the material. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves were recorded on a Q50 Thermo gravimetric Analyzer under pure N2 flow. The samples were heated from room temperature (25 °C) to 800 °C with a sample weight of 5–10 mg. The fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367-11), which was purchased from Hamamatsu (Japan).

MgSO4 (1.8 g 15 mmol), AlCl3·6H2O (1.2 g 5 mmol) and TbCl3·6H2O (1.1 g 3 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water then adjusted to pH = 9–10 by adding urea under continuous stirring. The above solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 90 °C overnight. The hydrous products were collected via filtration and washed with deionized water. Finally, the water was removed in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of –NH2 terminated LDH (aminated LDH named LDH-NH2)
ce:section.ce:para: 500 mg of crude LDHs was added into a reaction flask containing 150 mL of toluene. Then the mixture underwent sonication in an ultrasound bath at room temperature for 20 min to achieve homogenous LDH dispersion. Finally, an extremely fine, white gel was harvested. Next, 10 mL APTES was added to a flask at 60 °C under a N2 atmosphere stirred for 6 h. The precipitated solid was separated by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 3 min. Finally, the solid was dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of LDH-Cl intermediate (surface modified with -Cl group)
ce:section.ce:para: The Cl-containing intermediate (LDH-Cl) was introduced onto the surface of LDH-NH2 through an amidation reaction. Briefly, 450 mg of LDH-NH2 and 1 mL of TEA were dissolved in 35 mL dry toluene, Similarly, to the previous synthesis the mixture was subjected to sonication for approximately 10 min to achieve a homogenous system. 100 mg of 4-(chloromethyl) benzoyl chloride dissolved in 5 mL of toluene were subsequently slowly injected into a flask in an ice-bath. After 6 h, the LDH-Cl intermediate was isolated via centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 3 min and then rinsed with toluene three times to remove any excess 4-(chloromethyl) benzoyl chloride. The final products were dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

LDH-CTA was obtained by dissolving LDH-Cl (400 mg) and sodium diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate (500 mg) into 35 mL of methanol. Then this system was transferred into a dark box and stirred overnight. Finally, the reaction system was filtered and washed with deionized water several times. The final product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The preparation of hydrophilic LDHPEG composites was carried out through the new method involving metal-free photoinduced RAFT polymerization. In brief, 300 mg of LDH-Cl and1 g of PEGMA was added into 20 mL of dry toluene. Then the mixture underwent three freeze-pumpthaw circles to ensure the reaction was full of N2 or another inert gas. The RAFT polymerization was initiated by irradiation with 380 nm LEDs as the power source for the photocatalytic process. Approximately 4 h later the end-product was harvested after centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 3 min. The resulting products were dried at 60 °C overnight

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the cell viability of LDH-PEG composites
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability was assessed to evaluate the possible biomedical application of LDH-PEG composites, and a cell counting kits 8 (CCK-8) assay using L929 cells was used [49–52]. L929 Cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5 × 104 cells per mL in 160 μL of the respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After 24 h of cell attachment, L929 cells were incubated with 20–160 μg mL−1 of LDH-PEG composites for 12 and 24 h. Then the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) three times to remove any uninternalized nanoparticles. After that, 10 μL of CCK-8 dye and 100 μL of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) cell culture media was added to each well and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Plates were then analyzed with a microplate reader (VictorIII, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of the dye absorbance were carried out at 450 nm, with the reference wavelength at 620 nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 was compared to the control (cells not exposed to nanoparticles), which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used for each control and to test the concentrations per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of the untreated controls. The results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD).

The cell uptake behavior of LDH-PEG nanoparticles was examined using a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) as described in our previous report. Briefly, L929 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 2 mM glutamine, 100 U mL−1 penicillin, and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin. The cell culture was maintained at 37 °C in a humidified condition with 95% air and 5% CO2 in the culture medium. The culture medium was changed every three days to maintain the exponential growth of the cells. On the day prior to treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1 × 105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with LDH-PEG at a final concentration of 100 μg mL−1 for 3 h at 37 °C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to remove the LDH-PEG nanoparticles and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at room temperature. Cell images were taken using a LCSM Zesis 710 3-channel (Zesis, Germany) with an excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

The successful grafting of PEGMA onto the surface of LDHs was confirmed by various characterization techniques. For example, the functional groups present in the unmodified LDHs and their composites were examined by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 , a new peak appears in the FT-IR spectra of LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG composites at approximately 2910 cm−1, which can ascribed to an alkane C-H stretching vibration. In addition, the peaks located at 1726 and 1648 cm−1 can be attributed to -COOR and -CONHR groups, respectively. The -COOR functional group belongs to PEGMA, while -CONHR was formed after the reaction between LDH-NH2 and 4-(chloromethyl) benzoyl chloride. Moreover, the peak intensity at approximately 1108 cm−1 was clearly enhanced in the spectrum of LDH-PEG compared that of LDH-CTA. This was attributed to the C-O stretching vibration, further evidencing the successful introduction of PEGMA onto the LDHs through RAFT polymerization. All the above results provide strong evidences that the amino groups and PEGMA were successfully introduced onto the luminescent Tb3+-doped LDHs.

Consistent with the FT-IR results, the successful introduction of PEGMA onto the surface of Tb3+-doped LDHs was also confirmed by TGA. As is illustrated in Fig. 2 , a gradual weight loss was observed in the TGA curve of Tb3+-doped LDHs. The final weight loss of the unmodified Tb3+-doped LDHs is approximately 25.67%. This could be ascribed to the dehydration the LDHs. The weight loss increased to 35.4% in the LDH-NH2 sample. This change in total weight loss between of unmodified Tb3+-doped LDHs and LDH-NH2 indicated that APTES was successfully immobilized onto the surfaces of the LDHs. In addition, the weight loss increased further in the LDH-Cl and LDH-CTA samples, implying that CTA was conjugated onto the surface of LDHs. Moreover, the weight loss of the LDH-PEG composites increased significantly to 61.55%. This notable weight loss occurred from 310 to 420 °C, and can likely be ascribed to the decomposition of the polymers on the surface of the LDHs. This clearly indicated that PEGMA was grafted onto the surface of the LDHs through photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. Based on the TGA curve of LDH-PEG, the mass percentage of polymers on the surface of the modified LDHs was calculated to be approximately 29%. Therefore, we concluded that the Tb3+-doped luminescent LDHs was be successfully functionalized through the photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization.

XRD patterns of MgAlTb-LDH and LDH-PEG were collected from 2θ = 2°–80°, as in previous work we found that the XRD pattern of MgAlTb-LDH was similar to classical MgAl-LDH which indicated that Tb3+ replaced some Al3+, but still showed a typical LDH structure[53,54]. In Fig. 3 A, an A (0 0 3) peak appears at 2θ = 11.6° which corresponds to an interlayer distance of 0.75 nm, There is also an (0 0 3) peak in Fig. 3B at the same location which indicate that the interlayer distance did not change. Finally, after MgAlTb-LDH was modified with PEG, the XRD pattern became irregular. Specifically, the degree of crystallinity in LDH-PEG was inferior to that of MgAlTb-LDH, which can be proved from the change in intensity of the characteristic (0 0 3) peak, We hypothesize this change in crystallinity is attributed to the PEGMA grafted onto the LDH matrix.

Furthermore, TEM images were used to characterize the sizes and morphologies of the LDHs and LDH-PEG composites. We observed that the unmodified LDHs were stacked layer by layer and their sizes ranged from 120 to 180 nm. In Fig. 4 A, a laminated structure was clearly observed based on the TEM image. Meanwhile, LDH sheets were stacked, and LDHs could be observed even at the bottom, which indicates that Tb3+-doped LDH was ultrathin. However, unfortunately, there is little difference between Fig. 4A and B, which may because the superficial area of the LDH is too large to observe any structural change. Although many polymers were grafted onto the LDH surface, there is not different a large enough between LDH and LDH-PEG.

XPS is a powerful tool for studying the surface composition of materials, and was employed to provide evidence for the successful modification of Tb3+-doped LDH with PEGMA through surface-initiated controlled living polymerization. The survey XPS spectra from 0 to 1400 eV were examined and the results are displayed in Fig. 5 . Elements including C, N, O, Cl, S, Mg, Al and Tb were found in the survey XPS spectra. The major elements are O, Mg and C. In addition, some other elements such as Cl, Al, S and N were also found. Detailed XPS spectra of the Tb3+-doped LDHs and their composites are also displayed in Fig. S1 and Fig. 5. The Al 2p, Mg 1s, Tb 3d and O 1s spectra are shown in Fig. S1. The appearance of Tb in the samples directly confirmed that Tb was doped into the LDHs samples. Additionally, the emergence of S and Cl in the Tb3+-doped materials likely originated from the precursors in the LDH synthesis. The above results clearly indicated that we successfully obtained the Tb3+-doped luminescent LDHs through hydrothermal treatment. It is worth noting that C and N were also found in the Tb3+-doped LDHs. These elements likely originated from the urea. More importantly, the changes in the intensities of the peaks be used to confirm the successful surface modification with CTA and the polymers. For example, changes in the intensity of the N 1s peak was found in the Tb3+-doped LDHs, LDH-Cl and LDH-CTA samples. And the N likely derived from urea and APTES. After the surface of the LDH was functionalized with PEGMA, the intensity of the N 1s was markedly decreased. This change suggests implied that the PEGMA was successfully grafted onto the surface of the LDHs through RAFT polymerization.

The detailed XPS spectra of LDHs and their composites are also displayed in Fig. 6 . As seen in Fig. 6A, all samples showed peaks in the range of 284 to 287 eV. The C from LDH-Cl, LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG was certainly derived from Sodium 4-(chloromethyl) benzoyl chloride, diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate and PEGMA, while the C from the Tb3+-doped LDHs should be come from the urea that was used to adjust the pH. However, one other visible band was observed in LDH-PEG from 287 to 288 eV, which may be attributed to the C-O-C structure contributed by the PEGMA. This provides strong evidence that PEGMA was successfully grafted onto the surface of the LDH through the designed strategy. In addition, because the intensity at 287 eV was triple that at 285 eV, we can conclude that a mass of organic hydrophilic moieties were integrated with the inorganic matrix which will undoubtedly improve the water-dispersibility of the LDH matrix. Fig. 6B shows the N 1s spectra of the samples. All samples have a peak at the same binding energy, indicating the chemical environment of the N is similar across the samples. However, it can be observed that the intensity of N clearly increased after the surface of LDHs were immobilized with CTA. This implied that APTES was successfully immobilized on the surface of the LDHs. On the other hand, the intensity of the N 1s peak decreased in the LDH-PEG sample. This decrease in the N content suggests that the PEGMA was grafted on LDHs through surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. The S 2p XPS spectra of the LDHs and their composites are shown in Fig. 6C. The S in the Tb3+-doped LDHs mainly comes from SO4 2-, while the content of S decreased after APTES and diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate were introduced. Compared with the sample of LDH-Cl, the content of S increased dramatically again in LDH-CTA. This is possibly caused by a reaction between sodium diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate and LDH-Cl. This suggested that CTA was successfully immobilized on the surface of the LDHs. Moreover, the content of S decreased in the LDH-PEG samples, compared to that of LDH-CTA. This provides strong evidence for the successful grafting of PEGMA onto the surface of the LDHs through metal free photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. The Cl 2p spectra are illustrated in Fig. 6D, LDH-Cl contains abundant –Cl, which likely originated from 4-(chloromethyl) benzoyl chloride. The Cl content decreased sharply in LDH-CTA and LDH-PEG. This also implied that diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate and PEGMA were introduced on the surface of the LDHs. Therefore, based on the XPS results, we could deduce that PEGMA was facilely grafted onto the surface of these Tb3+-doped LDHs through the metal free photoinduced RAFT polymerization.

The relative elemental contents of C, N, O, S, Cl, Mg, Al and Tb were calculated based on the XPS spectra and the results are listed in Table 1 . The contents of C and N were 29.36% and 1.83% in the LDHs. However, the contents of C and N increased to 61.27% and 5.19% in the LDH-Cl sample. Simultaneously, the contents of S, Mg, and Al all decreased correspondingly in the LDH-Cl samples. The above results implied that APTES was immobilized on the LDHs. It is worth to noting that the S content increased from 0.75% to 2.59% after the surface of the LDH-Cl was modified with CTA. The increased of S content clearly indicated that LDH-CTA was synthesized successfully. Compared to the content of S in LDH-CTA, the content of S significantly decreased to 1.33% in the LDH-PEG sample. This indicated that the PEGMA monomer was grafted onto LDHs through photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. Based on all of the above comparisons, we could confirmed that the functional polymers could be successfully grafted on these luminescent LDHs through the method developed in this work. It’s worth to mention that the contents of Tb3+ is very low based XPS analysis and it is incompatible with original input of TbCl3·6H2O. to explain this problem, we collected the Tb3+compositions of LDH and LDH-PEG based on inductively coupled plasma (ICP) and the results were shown in Table S1. the results of ICP indicate that the Tb3+ content is abundant rather than 0.1% as XPS shown us, we guess that the Tb3+ replace Al3+ randomly or it were don’t arranged on the surface so that XPS can’t measure precisely.

To verify that the PEGMA modified Tb3+-doped LDH maintained a steady green fluorescence, the fluorescence spectra of the LDH-PEG composites were measured. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 , the excitation and emission spectra of LDH-PEG were obtained. The excitation spectra were obtained with an emission wavelength of 545 nm. This corresponding to the 5D4 → 7F5 transition emission of Tb(III). As shown, three strong peaks were observed at 356, 372 and 379 nm, respectively. The peak at 356 nm was related to the 5L10 ← 7F6 transition, and peaks at 372 and 379 nm were attributed to the 5D3 ← 7F6 transitions. Under the optimal excitation wavelength of 356 nm, the emission spectrum was measured, and four emission peaks were observed from the spectrum. These peaks belong to emission from the 5D4 → 7F6, 5D4 → 7F5, 5D4 → 7F4 and 5D4 → 7F3 transition emission, respectively. The above results suggested that the LDH-PEG composites still maintained the fluorescence properties of unfunctionalized Tb3+-doped LDHs. The fluorescence can potentially be used for cell imaging.

The water stability of LDH-PEG composites is another critical property for their applications in biomedical fields. The relative dispersibility of water suspensions of unmodified Tb3+-doped LDHs and LDH-PEG composites is shown in Fig. S2. Both unmodified Tb3+-doped LDHs and LDH-PEG composites could be well dispersed in water after ultrasonic treatment. The Tb3+-doped LDHs quickly precipitated 2 min after deposition and completely precipitated within 20 min. However, the water suspension of the LDH-PEG composites remained well-dispersed after 20 min. This improved water dispersibility implied that PEGMA was grafted onto the surface of the Tb3+-doped LDHs through photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization. The introduction of PEGMA could not only improve the water dispersion of the LDH-PEG composites, but could also change their pharmacokinetics behavior in vivo and decrease their accumulation in the reticulo-endothelial system. This is very important in improving the performance of LDHs based composites in biomedical applications.

Good biocompatibility and biodegradability are very important properties for the biomedical application of these materials. In this work the cell viability of LDH-PEG composites towards L929 cells was evaluated based on a CCK-8 assay. The results are displayed in Fig. 8 . Only a slight decrease in cell viability was detected at the tested concentrations. In particularly, the cell viability is almost unchanged after incubation with less than 80 μg mL−1 of the LDH-PEG composites. The cell viability remained higher than 90% even at concentrations of LDH-PEG composites up to 160 µg mL−1. The cell viability results give preliminary evidence of the good biocompatibility of LDH-PEG composites and supports their great potential in biomedical applications. This good biocompatibility is reasonable due to the high hydrophilicity and low toxicity of PEGMA. Moreover, it is well-known that the LDHs are primarily composed of inorganic components, meaning they could be potentially degradable in biological systems. Therefore, the high water dispersibility, desirable biocompatibility and potential biodegradability provide these luminescent LDH-PEG materials with great potential for biomedical applications.

The cell uptake behavior of LDH-PEG composites was examined by CLSM in order to evaluate their applicability for biological imaging. As shown in Fig. 9 A, fluorescence signals from the cell plates could be observed after irradiation with 488 nm laser. The fluorescence signals could form spindles with sizes of approximately 50 μm in length. At the center of these fluorescent areas, some dark areas could also be detected. These areas with intense fluorescence signals show the locations of the cell, while the dark areas are likely the locations of the cell nuclei. Moreover, the bright field image in Fig. 9B shows that the cell can adhere strongly to the cell plates and maintain their normal morphologies. Some of the areas with intense contrast are likely aggregates of the LDH-PEG composites. The bright field images further indicate that the LDH-PEG composites have low cytotoxicity at the concentrations used for cell imaging. Fig. 9C shows an overlay of the fluorescent image and bright field image. The locations of cells completely matched the fluorescence signal areas. In other words, the fluorescence signals were exactly overlaid on the cells. However, the fluorescence intensity in the core of the cells was too weak to be observed. This implied that the LDH-PEG composites mainly accumulated in the cytoplasm and could not enter the cell nuclei. This cell uptake behavior suggested that LDH-PEG composites have a high potential to be used as fluorescent probes for biological imaging and imaging guided drug delivery applications.

In summary, we report the surface modification of Tb3+-doped LDHs with hydrophilic polymers, which was developed through a photoinduced surface-initiated RAFT polymerization in the absence of catalyst using PEGMA as the monomer. The resultant LDH-PEG composites display excellent water dispersibility, low toxicity and can be potentially used for cell imaging, in addition, they have great potential to be used to monitor cell morphology and trace drug carriers (if the materials were further modified with drug carrier groups). Most importantly, compared with traditional controlled living radical polymerization, the photoinduced RAFT polymerization does not require no metal catalysts or initiators throughout the reaction, and can be run at room temperature. This approach not only elegantly avoids the toxicity and fluorescence quenching effect of transition metal catalysts, but also overcome the drawbacks s synthesis and conjugation of traditional CTA [55,56]. Furthermore, this method might also be attractive for the surface modification of some biomolecules, as it can be conducted below room temperature. Based on the characterization results, we demonstrated that the modified LDHs still maintain their fluorescence and could be effectively internalized by cells through non-specific internalization. Many other nanomaterials based polymer composites could also be fabricated by the monomers and functional composites. Therefore, this method should be expanded for the fabrication of other functional materials for different applications. For example, it would be interesting to fabricate polymer-modified functional hybrid materials for drug delivery or antibiosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014, 51673107, 21788102), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.08.033.
